skill.name: infrastructure
skill.description: Infrastructure as Code patterns for deploying Guts nodes using Terraform, Docker, and Kubernetes
skill.summary: Infrastructure as Code patterns for deploying Guts nodes using Terraform, Docker, and Kubernetes
skill.author: AbdelStark

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged Markdown code fences, Dockerfile commands, weak cryptography keywords, recursive deletion, and reconnaissance patterns. Review found no malicious intent, no prompt injection, and no runtime code that executes commands. The remaining risk is low because users may copy infrastructure examples that run package installation or cloud deployment commands.

low_findings.title: External Command Examples Are Documentation Only
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged Markdown fence markers as Ruby or shell backtick execution. These markers delimit Terraform, Dockerfile, Docker Compose, and Kubernetes examples, and they do not execute inside the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are Markdown code fence delimiters, not language runtime backticks. The surrounding content is static documentation for infrastructure examples.

low_findings.title: Scoped Docker Cache Cleanup Is Not Root Deletion
low_findings.description: The recursive delete finding points to Dockerfile cleanup of package manager lists under /var/lib/apt/lists. This is a common image-size reduction pattern and does not target root or home directories.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command deletes only apt cache files inside a container image layer. There is no evidence of deletion against /, /home, or user-controlled paths.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Detections Are Keyword Matches
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography findings appear to match substrings inside ordinary words such as description, accessModes, or service metadata. No cryptographic algorithm usage is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found no MD5, SHA-1, DES, RC4, or other weak cryptographic implementation. The flagged locations are prose or Kubernetes and Terraform field names.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Detection Is Terraform Metadata
low_findings.description: The reconnaissance finding points to a Terraform AMI attribute in an example AWS instance resource. It does not collect host information or exfiltrate environment details.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line assigns an Amazon Machine Image value for a Terraform resource. There is no command, network request, or host inventory logic.

content.value_statement: Deploying decentralized Guts nodes requires consistent infrastructure patterns across local, test, and production environments. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through Terraform, Docker, and Kubernetes examples.
actual_capabilities: Outlines Terraform module structure for network, compute, storage, and environment folders.
Shows an AWS instance module pattern for deploying multiple Guts nodes.
Provides a multi-stage Dockerfile example for building and running a Rust Guts node.
Defines a Docker Compose setup for three local development nodes.
Provides a Kubernetes StatefulSet pattern with persistent storage for node data.
Lists monitoring and security checklist items for production planning.
limitations: The examples are templates and require project-specific variables, images, and provider settings.
It does not include complete Terraform providers, backends, or Kubernetes service manifests.
It does not validate cloud costs, account permissions, or production capacity needs.
It assumes users review commands before applying infrastructure changes.

use_cases.title: Bootstrap Local Node Clusters
use_cases.description: Create a Docker Compose plan for several Guts nodes with peer configuration and persistent volumes.

use_cases.title: Plan Cloud Infrastructure Modules
use_cases.description: Design Terraform folders and module boundaries before building a repeatable deployment pipeline.

use_cases.title: Prepare Kubernetes Deployments
use_cases.description: Draft StatefulSet-based node deployments with storage, ports, monitoring, and security checklist items.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Local Cluster
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the infrastructure skill to outline a Docker Compose setup for three local Guts nodes. Include ports, peers, and volumes.

prompt_templates.title: Design Terraform Modules
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the infrastructure skill to propose a Terraform module structure for dev, staging, and production Guts environments.

prompt_templates.title: Draft Kubernetes Storage
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the infrastructure skill to design a Kubernetes StatefulSet plan for Guts nodes with persistent storage and stable networking.

prompt_templates.title: Review Production Readiness
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the infrastructure skill to review my Guts deployment plan for monitoring, TLS, secrets, network policies, and RBAC gaps.

output_examples.input: Plan a local Guts node cluster for development.
output: A three-node Docker Compose design with unique API ports.
Peer environment variables for node discovery.
Persistent named volumes for each node data directory.

output_examples.input: Design production infrastructure for Guts nodes.
output: A Terraform module layout separated by network, compute, storage, and environments.
A Kubernetes StatefulSet approach for node identity and persistent data.
A security checklist covering TLS, secrets, network policies, scanning, and RBAC.

best_practices: Review generated infrastructure before running Terraform, Docker, or Kubernetes commands.
Replace example image names, instance sizes, ports, and storage values with environment-specific settings.
Pair the deployment patterns with secrets management, monitoring, TLS, and access controls.
anti_patterns: Do not apply example Terraform directly to production without provider, backend, and cost review.
Do not use latest container tags for production releases without pinning and scanning images.
Do not expose node ports publicly without network policy and firewall controls.

faq.question: Does this skill deploy infrastructure automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides patterns and examples that users must adapt and execute separately.

faq.question: Which environments does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers local Docker Compose, test Kubernetes clusters, and production-oriented Terraform plus Kubernetes patterns.

faq.question: Does it include complete Terraform code?
faq.answer: No. It shows module structure and a sample node resource, not a complete provider configuration.

faq.question: Can it help with production security?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes checklist guidance for TLS, network policies, secrets, scanning, and RBAC.

faq.question: Is it specific to one cloud provider?
faq.answer: The production goal is cloud-agnostic Kubernetes, but one Terraform example uses an AWS instance resource.

faq.question: Should generated commands be run without review?
faq.answer: No. Users should review all generated infrastructure changes before running deployment commands.
